speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three with Elizabeth Gorski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the children in the ghetto begging for food and snatching food. Can you continue on with that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, there was this particular group, I remember. Three little kids, a boy and two girls. The oldest of them might have been eight or nine, no more than that. And they were coming to our yard every other day always with the same song and always with the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] or 'have mercy on us.' And one day, they stopped coming. People were dying in the street more and more. They were just falling dead. And every morning, handcarts, hand-pulled carts, were collecting the bodies and taking them to the cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My Aunt [? Maria ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] caught typhus in December '41. She was very, very ill. And even though-- because they were both doctors they had all the medication available then, she died on the 31st December 1941, which was the day her daughter [? Janina ?] sixth birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [? Janina ?] stayed with us for a while. We were then-- I forgot to say we had then moved from that flat we had occupied with that grandmother and uncle to another flat in [INAUDIBLE] Street. That part of [? Elektoralna ?] where we had lived previously had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: --there with my grandmother-- [INAUDIBLE] --the two small rooms. Hunger was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --mentally very unstable. Was it hunger? Was it the fact that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and she hadn't heard the word from him-- In those conditions, people could still sell and buy real estate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then some food started appearing on-- on our table. We-- That was barley and sometimes a potato and bones. Mother would buy horse blood. The only meat available was horse meat. And they were-- --really, even horses, they were old, dying hags. And she would make a pate out of it, out of horse blood. Wanda, little Wanda was a delight. She would-- would be sent downstairs to play in the yard, all by herself. She was about two, two and a half then. And when time would come for her to come home, [? Gilda ?] would call her through the window, and Wanda would walk upstairs on the first-- to the first floor all by herself. The yard was always indescribably dirty and muddy, but Wanda had never even dirtied her fingers. She was a real war child. She was never crying. She was always serene and smiling. And my mother loved her like her own, better than her own, I think. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What I remember those times is that it was getting more and more terrifying what the future held. Grandma was sick. She had a continuing colic. She could hardly get out of bed. She twice tried to commit suicide in my presence. And I screamed and called Mother. And later I thought, maybe I should have let her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But, of course, it was a very primitive way of attempt, and she couldn't have succeeded. She was just trying to strangle herself with a-- with a scarf. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The streets were becoming worse and worse and more dangerous. There were more and more sights like German officers stopping the Jews and making them practice sit-ups or make them dance in the street and beating them and they-- and trying to cut their beards away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the other hand, there were, all this time, cafes open, orchestras playing, people drinking and eating. It was quite incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which people would be eating and drinking at the cafes?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who were doing illegal business. They had untold amounts of money. There were those two Jewish businessmen in the ghetto. One was called [? Konn. ?] The other was called [? Heller. ?] There was no means of transport within the ghetto. The only tram line went just past the wall. They got hold somewhere of a very old-- this used tram and attached to it two horses and carried people through the streets of ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Polish word for horse is kon. The two men's names were [? Konn ?] and [? Heller. ?] We called those horse-drawn trams [? Konn-Hellerky. ?] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But really life was not funny there at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My Aunt Anna, as a doctor, worked in the Jewish hospital in the ghetto. She was always helping people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'll have to come back to her when we talk about the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] evacuation of the Warsaw ghetto, which will be very soon. But Aunt was a good friend of Dr. [? Korczak ?] and especially his right-hand lady, Stefania Wilczynska. [? Korczak ?] had in the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his children's home. And I remember, one day, Anna took us, my mother and I, to a show the kids there were putting on. It was quite incredible. Those kids were starved, yet they put a-- a show of-- you know, that's entertaining people. It was quite an experience for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the show was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember that. But I remember that it made quite an impression on me, the fact that they did that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [? Janina, ?] my young cousin, who was left motherless, was sent out of the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My aunt found a Polish lady who wanted to take her, and she was sent to-- to be sheltered with this Polish woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how she managed to make that contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. I don't. I don't know how. I must say that both Aunt Anna and my mother, [? Hele ?] were very determined, very tough, and very resourceful ladies. But, also, my mother had a number of friends from her school years who were in the so-called PPS, which was the Polish Socialist Party. And that was a group of Poles who were all for helping the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My aunt was a widow of a Bund activist, so also Bund was affiliated to the Polish Socialist Party. So she also had friends there. So I think she would have gone-- done that through-- through her contacts. But I never really knew any details. I never asked too many questions, because I felt that nobody was coming forward with answers. It was better not to know than to know too much then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was then-- that was what? '41, '42. I would've been 15. It was still better not to know. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There were times, should I say. Well, in '42-- first of all, let's go back. '41, the war with Russia start. Hitler invaded Russia. So then Wilno became part of German-occupied territory. And we thought that my father and brother would come back then, but somehow it has not eventuated. I don't really know why. I know that mother felt at one stage that maybe they didn't quite want to come back, that maybe Father found someone there, some attachment. Anyway, they stayed in Wilno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very sporadic. Very sporadic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then about August '42, the action of emptying the Warsaw ghetto began. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The troops brought to operate then were the most terrifying black-clad SS from the Baltic countries. They were the worst, I think, I have ever seen. They were the ones who were smashing babies' heads against walls, who were shooting anyone in sight, who would move out when they-- when they wanted them to. They would just come into a yard of this big apartment building and yell, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- everybody out-- and then ran through the flats shooting anyone who didn't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw this happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw this happening, yes. A few weeks later, Mother-- I don't know how. There again, I didn't ask questions. She wouldn't have answered them anyway. Mother organized for us to get out of the ghetto. You had then pay off three sets of police. Inside the gate was the Jewish police. At the gate itself were German policemen or soldiers. Outside the gate were Polish policemen. They all three had to be bought off. I don't know how she had arranged this, how she had any money. There was another way of getting out of the ghetto that some people used. There was the building of Warsaw's central court that had the entrance on both sides. You could enter from the ghetto and just walk through the courts and walk out on the outside. You just had to find a way of pulling off your sleeve strip with the Star of David. But my mother choose this way. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was absolutely terrified. We had to go singly. And I went out first. It-- it took some hour, and I was waiting in a doorway on the other side for her to come through, and I never knew what was happening. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was really completely terrified then. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would like here to come back to something that has given me much disquiet throughout my later years. My mother left her-- her mother, who was ill, who was old and in poor health to take me out. How did she live with that to the end of her days, I don't know. But I guess she had the choice. She could stay or take me. Maybe she could have sent me out by myself, but she did not want to let me off her-- off her side. She had already lost her son. I was the only one left. Aunt Anna stayed in the ghetto. She had sent her son, [? Yanik, ?] out to a Polish orphanage. Her daughter, Alina, was in the nursing school in the ghetto. They both stayed till quite late. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anna was-- because the Jewish hospital was very close to the Umschlagplatz. It's the spot where the freight trains were, where Jews were loaded into the freight trains. Anna was there pulling out as much as she could people that she knew in the last moment. When [? Gilda ?] was taken, she saw her with little [? Janina, ?] and she wanted to take the child from her. But [? Gilda ?] wouldn't give her. She said, wherever I go, she goes. And that's how they went. Grandma was eventually shot in her bed. We only learned of it a lot later. Aunt Anna and Alina left the ghetto later. I was sitting here last week talking to my cousin [? Janina ?] about those times and preparing my notes for the interview. And we both thought, well, there's so-- quite a few people saved in our family. We really were very fortunate. Yes, I think we are fortunate, but we are mainly fortunate because we had those two very strong and very resourceful women, Anna and [? Hele, ?] who managed to save two children of Anna's and myself and [? Janina, ?] who was an orphan by then and whose father had disappeared. We never knew where. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I go back here to the ghetto for a moment? The times when ghetto was being emptied, we saw endless groups of people walking to the trains through the streets. They were the beginning, I think, pieces of bread and some amount of jam in a paper for the road. Later, there was not even-- not even that.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I saw [? Korczak-- ?] [? Korczak ?] and Stefania Wilczynska leading their children to the train. Well, actually, the trains were far away from where we were, but we saw them walking through the street, and that's where they were going. Some of the children were carrying cabbage loaves. That was probably the only food they had left. And [? Korczak ?] had apparently told his kids they were going for a picnic. And they carried all the cabbage loaves that they had in stock. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mother and I had left the ghetto. It wasn't easy walking the streets of Warsaw. There were men-- mostly men, mostly youngish men-- looking at faces, searching for Jewish faces. I don't know if I looked Jewish, but I think that I always had very sad eyes. And that was one of the characteristics, especially then. So I was very, very frightened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When we first walked out, Mother took me to our old nanny [? Marinya's ?] mother, who was then a very old lady living somewhere in a small hamlet in a very old little house. And Mother left me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. By suburban, a sort of country train other than suburban. Mother left me there. I think she had to go back to organize hiding places for both of us and false documents. And there, as far as I know, her-- her Socialist friends had helped her. We both got false identity cards in two different names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember the day that you arrived at the nanny's mother's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember. It was sometime late September or early October '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she expecting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't stay there very long. Oh, she was quite willing to help us, but she was very old. And it was a small hamlet only. And I couldn't really go out of the house there. A strange girl in a place like this would stick out like a sore thumb. I couldn't show myself. So she was trying to give me some food to be good to me. She was a very nice old lady, but I was very, very frightened. I didn't know where my-- my mother was. I stayed there, I don't know, maybe 10 days or two weeks, and then I was taken to another place, a miller's house in the country. I was to look after the miller's son who had TB.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the miller?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- the son was actually older than I, but he was in a bad way already. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that this was meant to be a transitional place again. I don't think I could have stayed there for any longer. It wasn't easy in small country places where everybody knows everybody. A strange person arriving from somewhere is always suspicious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall where this miller's house was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was near Radzymin. Because I remembered that was the station we got out. And I stayed there another few weeks, and then mother came and took me to Czestochowa, the most Catholic city in Poland. In Czestochowa, I went to the house of a woman living on the outskirts of town. Unfortunately, I do not remember the woman's name. I would have loved to. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She looked after me for a few weeks. At that stage, were you concealing your identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew I was Jewish. So did [? Marinya's ?] mother, the old lady. I am not sure whether the miller did or not. I'm not sure. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother took you-- took you there, did she spend a night there or leave straightaway?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She would leave straightaway. She never stayed the night. She'd leave straightaway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she say to you when she left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all-- all she could say is, be careful. Don't talk. I stayed with this woman for another couple of weeks maybe. She was a very simple Polish woman. Uneducated. Catholic. She had a Jewish boyfriend, so she felt sorry for Jews. She apparently had helped quite a few Jewish children. She was eventually executed for it by the Germans. So was her boyfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to stay indoors the whole time you were with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to stay indoors there, yes, except that there was an outdoor toilet that I had to use. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She had her priest she was friendly with. And through him, she had found a place for me in a convent orphanage for girls. And I went there after a couple weeks or so with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she take you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The priest took me there. She didn't take me there herself. The priest took me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember that journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just walked. The orphanage was quite far from her house. It was at the feet of the famous hill where the most famous Polish Madonna is-- was situated. I don't think the nuns knew that I was Jewish. I don't believe it. They had, while I was there and at a later time they were writing to me, they had a number of times suggested that if I feel God's voice, I'm always welcome in the convent. But I'm quite sure that the priest must have known. And it was really quite frightening.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had to go to him to confession every week. And I kept thinking, does he know? He must know. What do I say? Do I keep confessing silly little things, or what do I have to say? I sort of kept behaving like there was nothing wrong with me, but it really was quite frightening. Were you pretending that you were a Catholic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was pretending that I was Catholic. I had then false documents of a Catholic girl. [INAUDIBLE]. My mother was pretending that she was my good mother. She stayed in Warsaw, but she visited me there once or twice, and she wrote letters to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, yes. I was called [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After some months, the convent had to move from the hill they were in, because the Germans took over this building, to another one. Life there was really weird. We were not really starving, but we were very scantily fed for months on. All we had was barley and onions.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here for a tape break.
